title: Gender Roles in the Church: Lesson 4 – 1 Timothy 2 and Women in the Church
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
source_files: Gender Roles in the Church - Lesson 4 - Elder Nick Anderson.txt

outline.title: Setting the Context: What 1 Timothy 2 Is and Is Not About
subpoints: The claim that Ephesus was a radical feminist city is historically unfounded—it was a typical Greco-Roman city
Paul's stated purpose in 1 Timothy (3:15) is not to address women specifically but to teach how to behave in the household of God
The fact that Paul addresses local problems at Ephesus does not make this passage culturally limited
Every letter Paul writes addresses particular problems, but the principles he grounds in creation order transcend culture

outline.title: Men and Women in Worship (vv. 8-10)
subpoints: Paul desires men to pray everywhere, lifting up holy hands without wrath and doubting—the emphasis is piety of heart, not posture
Women are commanded to dress modestly and with propriety, not with costly or attention-seeking adornment
Peter echoes the same concern: true beauty is the hidden person of the heart with a gentle and quiet spirit (1 Peter 3:3-4)
The sin is not in clothing itself but in the attitude of the heart; women are to adorn themselves with good works

outline.title: Women Learning and the Prohibition on Teaching (vv. 11-12)
subpoints: Paul's expectation that women will learn is itself countercultural—some in Judaism considered women's education sinful
Silence and submission describe a posture of learning, not a demeaning command—being a quiet learner is a positive attribute
The prohibition is two-fold: women are not to teach or to exercise authority over men
Refuting the argument that 'teach' only means passing on a fixed oral tradition: biblical examples show teaching always included explanation and application

outline.title: Grounds of the Prohibition: Creation and the Fall (vv. 13-14)
subpoints: The 'for' in verse 13 is causal—Paul is giving reasons, not illustrations, for the prohibition
First reason: Adam was formed first, establishing his role as leader, namer, and protector
Second reason: Eve was deceived and not Adam—when men and women swap roles, things go wrong
These grounds are rooted in creation order, not Ephesian culture—they apply universally

outline.title: Women Preaching: Evaluating the Dickson Argument
subpoints: John Dickson argues that 'teaching' (didaskō) in 1 Timothy 2:12 refers only to passing on a fixed oral apostolic tradition, not expository preaching
Under Dickson's definition, no one teaches anymore since the apostolic deposit is now written in the New Testament
Old Testament examples (Moses in Deuteronomy, Ezra in 2 Chronicles), the Sermon on the Mount, and Paul's instructions to Timothy and Titus all contradict this narrow definition
Teaching and authority are so closely linked grammatically in verse 12 that both must carry the same positive meaning

summary:
In this fourth lesson, Elder Nick Anderson provides a careful verse-by-verse examination of 1 Timothy 2:8-15, one of the most contested passages in the gender roles debate. He begins by dismantling the contextual argument that Paul is only addressing a locally unique problem: the claim that Ephesus was a radical feminist city is historically unsupported, and Paul's stated purpose in 1 Timothy 3:15 is to instruct about behavior in the household of God broadly, not to address a peculiarly Ephesian situation.

On verses 8-10, Elder Anderson notes that Paul's concern for men (praying with holy hands) and women (dressing modestly) is about piety of heart rather than external form. The sin is not in clothing itself but in the attitude it reflects—drawing attention to oneself rather than to God. Peter's parallel instruction in 1 Peter 3 confirms this reading.

On the core prohibition in verses 11-12, he observes that Paul's expectation that women will learn is itself countercultural given that some Jewish teachers considered women's education sinful. He then systematically refutes the three main attempts to soften the prohibition: (1) that the present tense of 'I do not permit' suggests it could change, (2) that 'teach' means only teaching error, and (3) that 'exercise authority' means only domineering rather than legitimate authority. Each argument is shown to be grammatically untenable or inconsistent with how the same words are used elsewhere in Scripture.

The lesson then addresses the 'Dickson argument'—the widely-read position of John Dickson that 'teaching' in this verse refers exclusively to transmitting the fixed oral apostolic tradition, not to expository preaching. Elder Anderson demonstrates that this definition is contradicted by Old Testament examples (Moses in Deuteronomy, Ezra and the Levites in 2 Chronicles, Jesus' Sermon on the Mount), Paul's own instructions to Timothy and Titus, and the plain logic of passages like 'all Scripture is profitable for teaching.' Under Dickson's definition, no one teaches anymore—a conclusion Dickson himself struggles to maintain consistently.

The lesson concludes with the causal grounds in verses 13-14: Adam was formed first (creation order) and Eve was deceived when she and Adam swapped roles (the fall). These are universal, not cultural, reasons for the prohibition.
transcript:
## Introduction and Opening Prayer

Well, let's open with a word of prayer. Gracious God, we thank You that we can be here tonight together to hear You and learn from Your word. Lord, we pray that You and working our hearts this morning. We pray that You would open us to Your word and what You have to say. We thank You, Lord, that You created us men and women different with different roles and different responsibilities. We pray, Lord, that You would help us understand those particular roles and that we fulfill those roles joyfully. If You would like to open your Bible, we would be looking at 1 Timothy chapter 2, verses 8 through 15.

1 Timothy 2:8-15:

"I desire therefore that the men pray everywhere, lifting up holy hands, without wrath and doubting; in like manner also, that the women adorn themselves in modest apparel, with propriety and moderation, not with braided hair or gold or pearls or costly clothing, but which is proper for women professing godliness, with good works. Let a woman learn in silence with all submission. And I do not permit a woman to teach or to have authority over a man, but to be in silence. For Adam was formed first, then Eve. And Adam was not deceived, but the woman being deceived, fell into transgression. Nevertheless she will be saved in childbearing if they continue in faith, love, and holiness, with self-control."

## Setting the Context

We're going to look through this passage in detail. The first thing we need to look at is some of the claims made about this passage.

The first claim is that Ephesus was a radical feminist city. And it sounds like that could be plausible. The only problem is that it's not true. What they say is: because this was a radical feminist city, Paul is really addressing that particular problem. Because it was unique to that city, this doesn't really apply to other cities and doesn't apply to us today. So we can safely ignore what Paul is saying here.

Ephesus was not a radical feminist city. It was pretty much a typical Greco-Roman city. The political, cultural, and religious elements were ordinary for their time. The magistrates were dominated by males. It was polytheistic, with most gods served by priests. In Acts, Luke described the main people in Ephesus—those who made shrines, high-ranking officials, the city clerk—and all of them were men. There's no indication that Timothy's congregation was a radical feminist congregation led by women.

Secondly, Paul was not writing especially to address women in the church. In 1 Timothy 3:15, he tells us his purpose: "so that you may know how one ought to behave in the household of God, which is the church of the living God." It's true that Paul addresses particular problems at Ephesus, but that's true of every letter he writes. There's nothing in Paul's letter that would indicate this passage is simply addressing a cultural issue.

## Men and Women in Worship (vv. 8-10)

Verse 8: "I desire therefore that the men pray everywhere lifting up holy hands without wrath and doubting." The emphasis here is not so much the posture—lifting up holy hands is not the only way to pray. It's the piety that Paul's getting at. The holiness, the attitude of the heart.

He then addresses women: "that the women adorn themselves in modest apparel, with propriety and moderation, not with braided hair or gold or pearls or costly clothing, but which is proper for women professing godliness with good works."

The first command is to dress modestly, not with costly clothing. What Paul's really getting at is the attitude of the heart—they've not been given to centrality; they've not dressed in such a way to attract attention to themselves.

1 Peter 3:3-4: "Do not let your adornment be merely outward—arranging the hair, wearing gold, putting on fine apparel—rather let it be the hidden person of the heart, with the incorruptible beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit, which is very precious in the sight of God." Peter is concerned with the attitude of the person, that they be beautiful not on the outside but on the inside.

Some have tried to claim that the braids and gold are cultural, therefore everything Paul says after this is also cultural and not applicable to our time. But Paul is not really concerned with cultural things here—he's concerned that people are going to abuse these things, and people do.

## Women Learning and the Prohibition on Teaching (vv. 11-12)

Verse 11: "Let a woman learn in silence with all submission." The first thing I want to point out here is that Paul's expectation that women will learn is countercultural. In that day, there were people in Judaism who considered it sinful for a woman to be educated. When someone takes this passage and tries to say that men who think women should not be in authority are putting women down—no, we're actually raising women up, because we expect them to be educated and well-learned and to understand the gospel.

Furthermore, silence and quietness is not a demeaning term. Being a quiet learner is a positive attribute. This is not something that should be taken as being put down.

Also, we've seen from 1 Corinthians 11 and 14 that silence does not mean never ever talking in the church. Silence in this context really means that women are not to be teachers, but learners.

"And I do not permit a woman to teach or to have authority over a man, but to be in silence." (v. 12)

Now some will try to argue that the verb here—"I do not permit"—is a present tense verb, meaning Paul is saying, "right now I am not permitting, but I could permit it in the future." The only problem with this is that it's grammatically totally unreasonable. There are 111 present tense verbs just like this one in the pastoral epistles. If we interpreted all of them that way, then maybe God no longer desires all people to be saved (1 Timothy 2:4); maybe the mystery of godliness is no longer great (1 Timothy 3:16); maybe there is no longer great gain with godliness and contentment (1 Timothy 6:6). That does a great deal of violence to the text.

Some would also argue that "teach" doesn't mean teaching in general, but only teaching error. The only problems with that: (1) it's not what the verse says; (2) why would Paul warn women against teaching error and not also warn the men, especially when the false teachers he names by name elsewhere are all men?; (3) the word for teaching here is almost always used in a positive sense—teaching truth. Every other time it's used in the NT, it's about teaching the truth.

Some argue "exercise authority" means only "domineering." Problems: (1) Why warn women against domineering and not men?; (2) Teaching and authority are grammatically linked in this verse—both must carry either a positive or negative meaning. Since "teach" is positive, authority must also be positive; (3) The word for authority here (authentein) is only used once in the NT, and outside the NT it means "to rule" or "to control" or "to act independently"—it does not mean "domineering." There are actually two commands in view: women are prohibited from doing two distinct things—teaching and having authority over men.

## Grounds of the Prohibition: Creation and the Fall (vv. 13-14)

After stating the command, Paul gives the reasons. Verse 13: "For Adam was formed first, then Eve."

The word "for" tips us off that Paul is going to explain why he gave this command. The first reason: the order of creation. Adam was formed first and had a distinct set of roles and responsibilities—he is the one who names and tends and protects; he is the leader. Eve was formed second and has a different set of roles; she nurtures, helps, and supports. This is the way God designed it. When those roles get confused or mixed up or swapped, things go wrong.

Verse 14: "And Adam was not deceived, but the woman being deceived, fell into transgression."

This could be interpreted a couple of ways. One interpretation is that women are more easily deceived than men—I find this dubious, because there are plenty of deceived men. The more likely interpretation is that what happens when men and women swap their roles is that things get messed up. Adam was supposed to be the head, the one leading. He was there with Eve during the whole event (Genesis 3:6 indicates Adam was with her), but he was not leading. Eve was talking back and forth with the serpent while Adam stood passively by. When the man does not take the lead as head, things go wrong.

However we understand verse 14, this is not a cultural thing. There is nothing that would lead us to believe this is simply cultural and can be dismissed. This is grounded in the order of creation.

## Women Preaching: Evaluating the Dickson Argument

At the end of the book we're going through, there's an appendix that deals with this question: should a complementarian church allow women to give the sermon? Some complementarians say: yes, women shouldn't be ordained, male leadership at home and in the church is vital—but women can still give sermons, under the authority of the session.

The most influential book on this comes from John Dickson, an Australian pastor: "Hear Her Voice: A Case for Women Preaching." He focuses on verse 12 and argues that the word "teach" (didasko) refers only to the specific activity of preserving and laying down the tradition handed down by the apostles—the oral tradition that is now the New Testament. He says that this activity is different from explaining and applying a Bible passage in today's typical expository sermon.

His argument rests on four parts: (1) there are different speaking activities in the New Testament; (2) didasko is a technical term for transmitting only the fixed oral apostolic tradition; (3) teaching in the NT never means explaining or applying a biblical passage; (4) since the apostolic message is now intact in the NT, no one teaches anymore.

The problem with this interpretation is that when you explain it to people, they laugh. Because the Bible itself tells us what teaching is, and it isn't what Dickson says.

In Deuteronomy 4:11-14, Moses taught the people God's words. In the Septuagint (the Greek OT), the same word didasko is used—and what Moses did was not merely repeat the tradition but explain it. In 2 Chronicles, Ezra set his heart to study the law and to teach it—he would read from the law and then explain it and apply it, and the Bible calls that teaching. The Sermon on the Mount: Jesus took allusions to Old Testament passages and explained what they actually meant, and the Bible calls Him a teacher (rabbi means teacher). Paul says that elders must be able to teach—if Dickson's definition is correct, no elder can teach anymore since we have the NT. Paul says older women should teach younger women—surely that doesn't mean only passing on an oral apostolic tradition.

Dickson admits there's overlap between exhorting and teaching, and it's not easy to draw a definite line. So how could you hold to his narrow definition and be sure you're not crossing it? Instead, it seems much more biblical to believe that what the Bible says in 1 Timothy means what it says.

Preaching is not simply sharing stories with a moral. Good preaching is taking the scriptures and showing us what they mean, applying them, showing forth who Christ is. This involves teaching and carries authority. When a pastor preaches the Word of God, he is speaking as though Christ is speaking. That is why a woman is not permitted to teach or to have authority in the church.

## Conclusion

God of order and not of confusion, we thank You that You have given men and women roles. Graciously given to them. We pray, Lord, that we would embrace these roles as men—that we would be leaders, that we would take up that challenge, that we would lead our families, lead our churches, lead our children in true godliness. We pray for our women, Lord, that they would be submissive, that they would love You, that they would teach their children, and that the younger women would be taught by the older women. We pray, Lord, that You would bless our church and bless the rest of our day. We ask this in Christ's name. Amen.
